channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: After Virginia Redistricting Map Is Tossed, Democrats Search Desperately for a Response
item.description: A conversation involving House members from Virginia and the top House Democrat reflected the fury and desperation that has gripped the party after Friday’s ruling in the state.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:description: Representative Hakeem Jeffries of New York, the minority leader, speaking at the Capitol in Washington last month.

item.title: Fearing an All-Republican Governor’s Race, Democrats Push for New Primary Rules in California
item.description: The prospect of Democrats getting shut out of the general election for governor has spurred a 2028 effort to eliminate the state’s “top-two” open primary approach.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: Voters in California fill out primary election ballots.

item.title: One Big Headache for Politicians These Days: a Messy Digital Footprint
item.description: As a new generation of chronically online oversharers runs for office, many find themselves tripped up by past statements. Their response? Delete, distance, disavow.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Leo Dominguez
item.media:credit: (clockwise from top left) Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times, Elizabeth Frantz for The New York Times, Jose Juarez/AP, Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times, Aaron Schwartz/Reuters, Hannah Schoenbaum/AP

item.title: G.O.P. Fatigue in Iowa Strains the Republican Primary for Governor
item.description: Five candidates are battling for the nomination in the June 2 primary. The winner will oppose Rob Sand, a Democrat, in the November election.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: KC McGinnis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Campaign signs for various Republican candidates for office line a road at the Iowa Faith & Freedom Coalition’s Spring Kickoff in Clive, Iowa last week.

item.title: Mass Layoffs in Iran as Businesses Buckle Under Wartime Pressures
item.description: Iran was already struggling economically before 2026 brought widespread instability. A government-imposed internet shutdown has crippled an entire sector.
item.dc:creator: Leily Nikounazar and Arash Khamooshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Crowds inside the sprawling Grand Bazaar in Tehran on Saturday. Imports of goods have been affected by the war.

item.title: Trump Administration Is Considering Pausing the Federal Gas Tax to Lower Prices, Energy Secretary Says
item.description: The tax is a little over 18 cents a gallon for gasoline. With the average national price above $4.50, the measure will provide little relief.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times
item.media:description: The average national price for a gallon of gasoline was $4.52 on Sunday, according to the AAA motor club.

item.title: Lebanese Mourn Eight Members of One Family Killed in Israeli Strike
item.description: Israel has escalated attacks on Lebanon since Thursday, further unraveling a truce in its war with Hezbollah. Among those killed on Saturday was a 6-month-old infant, relatives said.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.media:description: Mourners paying their respects to the eight family members who were killed in an Israeli strike on southern Lebanon.

item.title: Vance or Rubio? Trump Muses on Successor as the ‘Kids’ Fill Bigger Roles.
item.description: Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio are boosting their profiles, generating speculation about who’s lining up for the 2028 presidential nomination.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Vice President JD Vance at the White House earlier this year.

item.title: What to Do About the White House Correspondents Dinner After an Attack?
item.description: A gunman’s attack at the April dinner has spurred more debate than usual about one of Washington’s most dissected rituals.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Bumiller
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and Melania Trump, the first lady, at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner before a gunman rushed a security checkpoint for the event.

item.title: With Commissioner Under Pressure, F.D.A. Opens Door to Flavored Vapes
item.description: Though illicit e-cigarettes have flooded in from China, the new policy could allow major tobacco companies to sell from prime shelf space at thousands of stores.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Justin J Wee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rates of adolescent vaping have fallen steadily in recent years.

item.title: How Is Pope Leo Shaping the U.S. Church? Bishops.
item.description: His appointments have focused on pastoral care and reflect the changing composition of Catholic pews and priests.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Dias
item.media:description: Pope Leo XIV has an opportunity to make personnel changes at the highest levels of the Roman Catholic Church.

item.title: U.S.-China Rivalry Reaches South American Skies
item.description: The U.S. has pressed Argentina and Chile to review two Chinese telescope projects in the Andean deserts. Astronomers are worried about setbacks to research.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola, Edward Wong and Sarah Pabst
item.media:description: The unfinished Chinese telescope at night at the Cesco observatory in El Leoncito, in Argentina’s San Juan Province.

item.title: Why the Trump-Xi Summit Matters, Even if Little Seems to Come of It
item.description: The war in Iran, trade, artificial intelligence and Taiwan are expected to be on the agenda. But expectations are modest.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: President Trump meeting with China’s leader, Xi Jinping, in Busan, South Korea, in 2025.

item.title: How China May Have Sought to Recruit a House Aide to Spy on the U.S.
item.description: A staff member on the House China Committee was promised $10,000 for U.S. policy insights, on issues like Venezuela and rare-earth minerals.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: The outreach appears to provide a vivid portrait of how Beijing’s spy services seek to gain access to information from within the corridors of power in Washington.

item.title: Cruise Ship Linked to Hantavirus Outbreak Arrives in Spain’s Canary Islands for Disembarking
item.description: Small groups of passengers and crew left the ship after it anchored off Spain’s Canary Islands on Sunday. People were expected to fly to their home countries through Monday, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Carlos Barragán, Amelia Nierenberg and Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: A passenger from the MV Hondius speaking with medical staff in the Granadilla Port in Tenerife, Canary Islands, on Sunday.

item.title: British Paratroopers Deliver Aid to Remote Islands With Possible Hantavirus Case
item.description: The British military took critical medical supplies to Tristan da Cunha, a tiny British archipelago where authorities have identified a suspected hantavirus case.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: Ministry of Defense, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A photo released by the British Ministry of Defense showed a parachuter landing on Tristan da Cunha, an archipelago of islands in the South Atlantic Ocean.

item.title: The Platner Trap
item.description: The exact wrong answer to a Republican Party that’s flirting with fascism is a man who chose to put vile Nazi imagery on his own body.
item.dc:creator: David French
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: For Some Patients, Cancer Is Becoming Like a Chronic Illness
item.description: For some patients, cancer is becoming like a chronic illness.
item.dc:creator: Daniela J. Lamas

item.title: Words of Wisdom
item.description: Today, we’re sharing some favorite bits of advice from moms.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Pearson

item.title: Putin’s Forces Are Barely Inching Along on the Battlefield
item.description: The Russian military has yet to solve a fundamental problem: how to make big advances in eastern Ukraine when drones are everywhere.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne, Cassandra Vinograd and Milana Mazaeva
item.media:description: An Ukrainian soldier in the besieged eastern city of Kostiantynivka early this year.

item.title: Beware of Wolves, but Straw Houses Could Help With Climate Change
item.description: Near Hudson, N.Y., a Princeton University team erected a cottage made from the agricultural byproduct, which they said is more sustainable than bricks or concrete.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times
item.media:description: A straw house built as an experiment stands 28 feet tall and has a sleeping loft, a daybed, five windows and a kitchen.

item.title: Rudy Giuliani Once Helped a Fellow Mayor Get Health Care
item.description: As the former mayor seeks help from the World Trade Center Health Program, an episode where he helped his predecessor John V. Lindsay carries resonance.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Nazaryan
item.media:description: Mayor Rudolph W. Giuliani and his predecessor John V. Lindsay in New York City in November 1993.

item.title: Keir Starmer Says No, He Won’t Resign
item.description: But if his opponents mounted a challenge, how would it unfold?
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain speaking to supporters and councilors in London on Friday, the day after elections were held in England, Wales and Scotland.
